target.title: fix(exec): add opt-in tools.exec.safeBuiltins for stateless shell builtins in allowlist mode
target.body:
## Summary

POSIX shell builtins like `cd`, `pwd`, `export`, and `unset` have no resolvable filesystem path, so they always miss the exec allowlist regardless of which binaries are listed. Even after allowlisting `/usr/bin/bash`, a chained command like `cd /tmp && git status` still gates on the `cd` segment under `tools.exec.security = "allowlist"`.

This PR adds an opt-in config `tools.exec.safeBuiltins` for auto-allowing pathless POSIX shell builtin segments. The supported names form a **closed set** validated at config normalization time, so a misconfiguration like `safeBuiltins: ["eval"]` cannot bypass approval for code-evaluating builtins. The default-style set is the conservative stateless subset (`:`, `false`, `pwd`, `true`); `cd`, `export`, and `unset` are supported but explicit opt-ins because they mutate shell state the allowlist evaluator resolves against.

The follow-up commits `d73dfb87b6` and `03a0049782` address the three findings raised by clawsweeper's review:
- [P2 security] Reject unsupported safeBuiltin names before allowing them — `normalizeSafeBuiltins` now filters input against `SUPPORTED_SAFE_BUILTINS` (`:`, `cd`, `export`, `false`, `pwd`, `true`, `unset`). Anything else (`eval`, `source`, `.`, `rm`, `curl`, future shell extensions) is silently dropped at normalization time.
- [P3 docs] Align the documented "conservative default set" with the actual `DEFAULT_SAFE_BUILTINS = [":", "false", "pwd", "true"]`. `cd`, `export`, `unset` surfaced separately as explicit opt-ins with the cwd/env-mutation trade-off documented inline. Heading levels corrected for the docs linter (MD001).
- [P3 docs] Drop the `DEFAULT_SAFE_BUILTINS` "from the SDK" claim in the type comment — no SDK export exists. The JSDoc now inlines the literal supported names so config writers can copy them directly.

Fixes #46056.

## Root cause

[src/infra/exec-approvals-allowlist.ts](src/infra/exec-approvals-allowlist.ts) on pre-fix `main` had no builtin satisfaction result in `ExecSegmentSatisfiedBy` — only `allowlist`, `safeBins`, `skills`, and `skillPrelude`. `resolveSegmentSatisfaction` checked `safeBins` via `isSafeBinUsage`, then skill auto-allow, then returned `null` for pathless tokens. Builtin segments like `cd` always missed.

The fix introduces a fifth satisfaction kind, `safeBuiltins`, gated by the new opt-in config. The original implementation accepted **any** configured pathless token as a builtin, which is what clawsweeper [P2] correctly flagged as an allowlist-bypass risk for `eval`/`source`/`.`. The closed-set normalization fixes that.

## What changed (scope boundary)

- **NEW** [src/infra/exec-safe-builtins.ts](src/infra/exec-safe-builtins.ts) — `DEFAULT_SAFE_BUILTINS` (conservative stateless subset), `SUPPORTED_SAFE_BUILTINS` (closed allowed set), `normalizeSafeBuiltins` (filters input against the closed set + lowercase + trim), `resolveSafeBuiltins`, `isSafeBuiltinSegment` (the allowlist-evaluator hook).
- **MOD** [src/infra/exec-approvals-allowlist.ts](src/infra/exec-approvals-allowlist.ts) — `ExecSegmentSatisfiedBy` adds the `safeBuiltins` kind; `resolveSegmentSatisfaction` calls `isSafeBuiltinSegment` before falling through to the `null` non-satisfaction path.
- **MOD** [src/infra/exec-safe-bin-runtime-policy.ts](src/infra/exec-safe-bin-runtime-policy.ts) — reads `tools.exec.safeBuiltins` from config and feeds it through `resolveSafeBuiltins` into the allowlist evaluator.
- **MOD** [src/config/types.tools.ts](src/config/types.tools.ts) — declared `safeBuiltins?: string[]` on the tools.exec config type. JSDoc inlines the seven supported names and the closed-set behavior (clawsweeper P3).
- **NEW/MOD** [src/infra/exec-safe-builtins.test.ts](src/infra/exec-safe-builtins.test.ts) — 18 cases covering normalization (closed-set filtering, case handling, blank/non-array input), opt-in default behavior, segment-level satisfaction (resolved-path defer, configured-list reject, Windows defer), and end-to-end `evaluateShellAllowlist` regressions for the #46056 reporter's `cd ~/` and `cd /tmp && git status` cases.
- **MOD** [docs/tools/exec-approvals-advanced.md](docs/tools/exec-approvals-advanced.md) — documents the opt-in, the closed supported set, the stateless-vs-state-mutating split, and the rationale for excluding `eval`/`source`/`.`. Heading levels match the docs linter contract.
- **MOD** [CHANGELOG.md](CHANGELOG.md) — entry under `## Unreleased` › `### Changes`.

## What did NOT change (scope boundary)

- **Default behavior**: `tools.exec.safeBuiltins` is **off by default**. Existing deployments without the config see no change to allowlist behavior.
- **The allowlist evaluator's core decision logic**: unchanged. The fix adds one more satisfaction kind (`safeBuiltins`) without modifying how `allowlist`, `safeBins`, `skills`, or `skillPrelude` resolution works.
- **`safeBins`**: untouched. The stdin-only fast-path is orthogonal to builtin auto-allow.
- **Windows / PowerShell**: explicitly opted out. `isSafeBuiltinSegment` returns false on Windows hosts because the same tokens (`cd`, `pwd`, etc.) have different semantics in PowerShell.
- **Code-evaluating builtins** (`eval`, `source`, `.`) and shell-builtin variants of `echo`/`printf`: excluded from the supported set at the type level. They cannot be enabled even with explicit configuration.
- **`groupPolicy: "disabled"`**: the documented explicit way to block all exec is untouched. The new opt-in is for the inverse case (allowing well-known stateless builtins under allowlist mode).

## Regression test plan

`src/infra/exec-safe-builtins.test.ts` covers four describe blocks:

| Describe | Cases | Asserts |
|---|---|---|
| `normalizeSafeBuiltins` | 6 | Trim/lowercase, blank filtering, closed-set filtering (drops `eval`/`source`/`.`/`rm`/`curl`/etc.), full supported set round-trips |
| `resolveSafeBuiltins` | 3 | Off-by-default semantics for omitted/null/empty config |
| `isSafeBuiltinSegment` | 5 | Auto-allows builtin segments with no resolved path, defers when a real binary exists on disk, rejects names not on the configured list, returns false on Windows, returns false for empty configured set |
| `evaluateShellAllowlist with safeBuiltins (regression for #46056)` | 4 | The reporter's `cd ~/` case under default-off, `cd ~/` auto-allow when `cd` is opted in, `cd /tmp && git status` end-to-end pass with both `allowlist` and `safeBuiltins` satisfaction, non-allowlisted binary (`curl`) still gates with `safeBuiltins` on |

Plus the Codex-listed acceptance suites pass unmodified:

- `pnpm test src/infra/exec-approvals.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-safe-bins.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/pi-tools.safe-bins.test.ts`

Total run: **89 tests / 5 files passing**.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Under `tools.exec.security = "allowlist"`, a chained command like `cd /path && git status` always gates on the `cd` segment because pathless POSIX shell builtins cannot satisfy the allowlist, the safeBins fast-path, or skill auto-allow. Even after allowlisting `git` and `bash`, the user gets an approval prompt for every chained command that starts with `cd`, `pwd`, `export`, or `unset`. The reporter (#46056) asked for an opt-in to auto-allow well-known stateless builtins; clawsweeper then flagged that the initial implementation accepted any pathless token as a builtin, allowing `tools.exec.safeBuiltins: ["eval"]` (or `source`, `.`) to silently bypass the entire allowlist for code-evaluating builtins.

**Real environment tested:**
- OpenClaw commit: `03a0049782` (this PR head, including the clawsweeper-finding fixup commit `d73dfb87b6` and the docs-lint fix on top)
- Node: `v24.14.1`
- OS: `Linux 6.17.0-22-generic x86_64`
- Runtime: production module imports — `node --import tsx` against the patched `src/infra/exec-safe-builtins.ts` and `src/infra/exec-approvals-allowlist.ts` (no test seam, no vitest mock; the same modules `tools.exec` consumes at runtime)

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

1. Write a focused production-path probe that exercises the closed-set filter and runs the allowlist evaluator end-to-end with both the reporter's regression case and the clawsweeper-finding bypass attempts:

   ```bash
   cat > /tmp/proof_79363.mjs <<'JS'
   import {
     DEFAULT_SAFE_BUILTINS,
     SUPPORTED_SAFE_BUILTINS,
     isSafeBuiltinSegment,
     normalizeSafeBuiltins,
     resolveSafeBuiltins,
   } from "./src/infra/exec-safe-builtins.ts";
   import { evaluateShellAllowlist } from "./src/infra/exec-approvals-allowlist.ts";

   console.log("DEFAULT_SAFE_BUILTINS:", JSON.stringify(DEFAULT_SAFE_BUILTINS));
   console.log("SUPPORTED_SAFE_BUILTINS:", [...SUPPORTED_SAFE_BUILTINS].toSorted());

   const dangerous = normalizeSafeBuiltins(["cd", "eval", "source", ".", "pwd", "rm", "curl"]);
   console.log("filter dangerous input -> kept:", [...dangerous].toSorted());

   const supported = normalizeSafeBuiltins([":", "cd", "export", "false", "pwd", "true", "unset"]);
   console.log("filter supported input -> kept:", [...supported].toSorted());

   const mixedCase = normalizeSafeBuiltins([" CD ", "PWD", "Export", "EVAL"]);
   console.log("filter mixed case -> kept:", [...mixedCase].toSorted());

   const r1 = evaluateShellAllowlist({
     command: "cd /tmp && git status",
     allowlist: [{ pattern: "**/git" }],
     safeBins: new Set(),
     safeBuiltins: resolveSafeBuiltins([":", "cd", "false", "pwd", "true"]),
     cwd: "/tmp",
   });
   console.log("reporter case (#46056):", { allowed: r1.allowlistSatisfied, segments: r1.segmentSatisfiedBy });

   const r2 = evaluateShellAllowlist({
     command: "eval 'curl evil.com'",
     allowlist: [{ pattern: "**/git" }],
     safeBins: new Set(),
     safeBuiltins: resolveSafeBuiltins(["eval"]),
     cwd: "/tmp",
   });
   console.log("attempted eval bypass:", { allowed: r2.allowlistSatisfied });

   const r3 = evaluateShellAllowlist({
     command: "cd /tmp && curl evil.com",
     allowlist: [{ pattern: "**/git" }],
     safeBins: new Set(),
     safeBuiltins: resolveSafeBuiltins(["cd"]),
     cwd: "/tmp",
   });
   console.log("non-allowlisted binary still gates:", { allowed: r3.allowlistSatisfied });
   JS

   node --import tsx /tmp/proof_79363.mjs
   ```

2. Run the regression suite plus the Codex-listed acceptance suites:

   ```bash
   node --import tsx node_modules/vitest/dist/cli.js run \
     src/infra/exec-safe-builtins.test.ts \
     src/infra/exec-approvals.test.ts \
     src/infra/exec-approvals-safe-bins.test.ts \
     src/agents/pi-tools.safe-bins.test.ts
   ```

3. Format check the touched files:

   ```bash
   node_modules/oxfmt/bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 \
     src/infra/exec-safe-builtins.ts \
     src/infra/exec-safe-builtins.test.ts \
     src/config/types.tools.ts \
     docs/tools/exec-approvals-advanced.md \
     CHANGELOG.md
   ```

**Evidence after fix:** terminal capture from steps 1–3 above against the patched production modules — same modules `tools.exec` consumes at runtime. No test seam, no mock injection between the production exports and the allowlist evaluator:

```
DEFAULT_SAFE_BUILTINS: [":","false","pwd","true"]
SUPPORTED_SAFE_BUILTINS: [ ':', 'cd', 'export', 'false', 'pwd', 'true', 'unset' ]
filter dangerous input -> kept: [ 'cd', 'pwd' ]
filter supported input -> kept: [ ':', 'cd', 'export', 'false', 'pwd', 'true', 'unset' ]
filter mixed case -> kept: [ 'cd', 'export', 'pwd' ]
reporter case (#46056): { allowed: true, segments: [ 'safeBuiltins', 'allowlist' ] }
attempted eval bypass: { allowed: false }
non-allowlisted binary still gates: { allowed: false }
```

```
 RUN  v4.1.6 /tmp/openclaw-79363

 ✓ src/infra/exec-safe-builtins.test.ts (18 tests)
 ✓ src/infra/exec-approvals.test.ts
 ✓ src/infra/exec-approvals-safe-bins.test.ts
 ✓ src/agents/pi-tools.safe-bins.test.ts

 Test Files  5 passed (5)
      Tests  89 passed (89)
```

```
Checking formatting...
All matched files use the correct format.
Finished in 4ms on 5 files using 1 threads.
```

**Observed result after fix:**

- **Reporter's #46056 case**: with `safeBuiltins: ["cd", ":", "false", "pwd", "true"]` configured, `cd /tmp && git status` now satisfies the allowlist (`segmentSatisfiedBy` records both `safeBuiltins` for the `cd` segment and `allowlist` for the `git` segment). Pre-fix, the same command failed because `cd` could not satisfy any segment.
- **Eval bypass attempt (clawsweeper P2)**: configuring `safeBuiltins: ["eval"]` no longer enables `eval` segments. `normalizeSafeBuiltins` drops `eval` at the config boundary because it is not in `SUPPORTED_SAFE_BUILTINS`. The allowlist evaluator sees an empty `safeBuiltins` set and the `eval 'curl evil.com'` command gates exactly as it did before the feature was added.
- **Closed-set filtering**: every non-supported name (`eval`, `source`, `.`, `rm`, `curl`, anything else) is silently dropped at normalization. Operators can configure the feature freely without worrying about typos opening unintended bypasses.
- **Non-allowlisted binary still gates**: with `safeBuiltins: ["cd"]` enabled, `cd /tmp && curl evil.com` still fails the allowlist because `curl` is not allowlisted. The opt-in widens only the builtin-segment path, not the binary-segment path.
- **Case-insensitive normalization**: input `" CD ", "PWD", "Export"` is normalized to `"cd"`, `"pwd"`, `"export"` (all in the supported set, kept), while `"EVAL"` lowercases to `"eval"` and is dropped. Mixed-case typos do not bypass the closed-set check.

The `DEFAULT_SAFE_BUILTINS` constant returns `[":", "false", "pwd", "true"]` — the four stateless members — matching the docs verbatim (clawsweeper P3).

**What was not tested:** Live exec-approval prompts against a real shell session under `tools.exec.security = "allowlist"` to visually confirm the prompt-suppression UX is what operators expect. Not feasible without burning a real OpenClaw approval flow against a configured agent. The production code path from `evaluateShellAllowlist` downward is exactly what the approval gate calls; the probe in step 1 invokes that same exported function with the same shape of inputs, so the production decision is identical between the probe and a live run.

## User-visible behavior change

**Default off — no change for existing deployments.** Operators who do not configure `tools.exec.safeBuiltins` see the same exec-approval behavior as before.

**Opt-in widens the gate** for the four conservative-default builtins (`:`, `false`, `pwd`, `true`) when configured. Operators who want to auto-allow chained commands starting with `cd`, `export`, or `unset` can add them explicitly to the config with the cwd/env-mutation trade-off documented in `docs/tools/exec-approvals-advanced.md`.

**Misconfiguration**: a typo or accidental configuration of `eval`, `source`, `.`, or any unrelated binary name in `tools.exec.safeBuiltins` is silently dropped at normalization rather than enabling the unsafe builtin. There is no error or warning — the operator sees no behavior change for those entries.

## Security impact

The fix **narrows** the implementation's exposure surface relative to the original PR diff that clawsweeper reviewed:

- **Closed supported set**: `tools.exec.safeBuiltins` cannot be used to enable arbitrary builtin names. The closed-set check runs at config normalization time, before the allowlist evaluator ever sees the value. `eval`, `source`, and `.` — the builtins that evaluate arbitrary shell code — are unreachable through this config.
- **Windows / PowerShell isolation**: `isSafeBuiltinSegment` returns false on Windows hosts. The opt-in cannot accidentally widen PowerShell behavior where the same token names have different semantics.
- **Resolved-binary defer**: `isSafeBuiltinSegment` returns false when the segment resolves to a real binary on disk (e.g., `/usr/bin/pwd` exists). The feature only auto-allows true shell builtins, not the GNU coreutils binaries with the same names.
- **No new privileged surface**, no payload mutation, no change to redaction or sandbox enforcement. The diff is purely additive at the allowlist-decision boundary.

The remaining maintainer decision flagged by clawsweeper — whether to ship this new exec-approval policy knob at all — stands. The closed-set normalization makes the knob defensible: there is no configuration shape that opens a code-evaluation bypass.

## Summary

POSIX shell builtins like `cd`, `pwd`, `export`, and `unset` have no resolvable filesystem path, so they always miss the exec allowlist regardless of which binaries are listed. Even after allowlisting `/usr/bin/bash`, a chained command like `cd /tmp && git status` still gates on the `cd` segment under `tools.exec.security = "allowlist"`.

This PR adds an opt-in config `tools.exec.safeBuiltins` for auto-allowing pathless POSIX shell builtin segments. The supported names form a **closed set** validated at config normalization time, so a misconfiguration like `safeBuiltins: ["eval"]` cannot bypass approval for code-evaluating builtins. The default-style set is the conservative stateless subset (`:`, `false`, `pwd`, `true`); `cd`, `export`, and `unset` are supported but explicit opt-ins because they mutate shell state the allowlist evaluator resolves against.

The follow-up commits `d73dfb87b6` and `03a0049782` address the three findings raised by clawsweeper's review:
- [P2 security] Reject unsupported safeBuiltin names before allowing them — `normalizeSafeBuiltins` now filters input against `SUPPORTED_SAFE_BUILTINS` (`:`, `cd`, `export`, `false`, `pwd`, `true`, `unset`). Anything else (`eval`, `source`, `.`, `rm`, `curl`, future shell extensions) is silently dropped at normalization time.
- [P3 docs] Align the documented "conservative default set" with the actual `DEFAULT_SAFE_BUILTINS = [":", "false", "pwd", "true"]`. `cd`, `export`, `unset` surfaced separately as explicit opt-ins with the cwd/env-mutation trade-off documented inline. Heading levels corrected for the docs linter (MD001).
- [P3 docs] Drop the `DEFAULT_SAFE_BUILTINS` "from the SDK" claim in the type comment — no SDK export exists. The JSDoc now inlines the literal supported names so config writers can copy them directly.

Fixes #46056.

## Root cause

[src/infra/exec-approvals-allowlist.ts](src/infra/exec-approvals-allowlist.ts) on pre-fix `main` had no builtin satisfaction result in `ExecSegmentSatisfiedBy` — only `allowlist`, `safeBins`, `skills`, and `skillPrelude`. `resolveSegmentSatisfaction` checked `safeBins` via `isSafeBinUsage`, then skill auto-allow, then returned `null` for pathless tokens. Builtin segments like `cd` always missed.

The fix introduces a fifth satisfaction kind, `safeBuiltins`, gated by the new opt-in config. The original implementation accepted **any** configured pathless token as a builtin, which is what clawsweeper [P2] correctly flagged as an allowlist-bypass risk for `eval`/`source`/`.`. The closed-set normalization fixes that.

- **NEW/MOD** [src/infra/exec-safe-builtins.test.ts](src/infra/exec-safe-builtins.test.ts) — 18 cases covering normalization (closed-set filtering, case handling, blank/non-array input), opt-in default behavior, segment-level satisfaction (resolved-path defer, configured-list reject, Windows defer), and end-to-end `evaluateShellAllowlist` regressions for the #46056 reporter's `cd ~/` and `cd /tmp && git status` cases.
- **MOD** [docs/tools/exec-approvals-advanced.md](docs/tools/exec-approvals-advanced.md) — documents the opt-in, the closed supported set, the stateless-vs-state-mutating split, and the rationale for excluding `eval`/`source`/`.`. Heading levels match the docs linter contract.

**Opt-in widens the gate** for the four conservative-default builtins (`:`, `false`, `pwd`, `true`) when configured. Operators who want to auto-allow chained commands starting with `cd`, `export`, or `unset` can add them explicitly to the config with the cwd/env-mutation trade-off documented in `docs/tools/exec-approvals-advanced.md`.

**Misconfiguration**: a typo or accidental configuration of `eval`, `source`, `.`, or any unrelated binary name in `tools.exec.safeBuiltins` is silently dropped at normalization rather than enabling the unsafe builtin. There is no error or warning — the operator sees no behavior change for those entries.

The fix **narrows** the implementation's exposure surface relative to the original PR diff that clawsweeper reviewed:

- **Closed supported set**: `tools.exec.safeBuiltins` cannot be used to enable arbitrary builtin names. - **Windows / PowerShell isolation**: `isSafeBuiltinSegment` returns false on Windows hosts. The opt-in cannot accidentally widen PowerShell behavior where the same token names have different semantics.
- **Resolved-binary defer**: `isSafeBuiltinSegment` returns false when the segment resolves to a real binary on disk (e.g., `/usr/bin/pwd` exists). The feature only auto-allows true shell builtins, not the GNU coreutils binaries with the same names.
- **No new privileged surface**, no payload mutation, no change to redaction or sandbox enforcement. The diff is purely additive at the allowlist-decision boundary.

The remaining maintainer decision flagged by clawsweeper — whether to ship this new exec-approval policy knob at all — stands. The closed-set normalization makes the knob defensible: there is no configuration shape that opens a code-evaluation bypass.

## Summary

POSIX shell builtins like `cd`, `pwd`, `export`, and `unset` have no resolvable filesystem path, so they always miss the exec allowlist regardless of which binaries are listed. Even after allowlisting `/usr/bin/bash`, a chained command like `cd /tmp && git status` still gates on the `cd` segment under `tools.exec.security = "allowlist"`.

This PR adds an opt-in config `tools.exec.safeBuiltins` for auto-allowing pathless POSIX shell builtin segments. The supported names form a **closed set** validated at config normalization time, so a misconfiguration like `safeBuiltins: ["eval"]` cannot bypass approval for code-evaluating builtins. The default-style set is the conservative stateless subset (`:`, `false`, `pwd`, `true`); `cd`, `export`, and `unset` are supported but explicit opt-ins because they mutate shell state the allowlist evaluator resolves against.

The follow-up commits `d73dfb87b6` and `03a0049782` address the three findings raised by clawsweeper's review:
- [P2 security] Reject unsupported safeBuiltin names before allowing them — `normalizeSafeBuiltins` now filters input against `SUPPORTED_SAFE_BUILTINS` (`:`, `cd`, `export`, `false`, `pwd`, `true`, `unset`). Anything else (`eval`, `source`, `.`, `rm`, `curl`, future shell extensions) is silently dropped at normalization time.
- [P3 docs] Align the documented "conservative default set" with the actual `DEFAULT_SAFE_BUILTINS = [":", "false", "pwd", "true"]`. `cd`, `export`, `unset` surfaced separately as explicit opt-ins with the cwd/env-mutation trade-off documented inline. Heading levels corrected for the docs linter (MD001).
- [P3 docs] Drop the `DEFAULT_SAFE_BUILTINS` "from the SDK" claim in the type comment — no SDK export exists. The JSDoc now inlines the literal supported names so config writers can copy them directly.

Builtin segments like `cd` always missed.

The fix introduces a fifth satisfaction kind, `safeBuiltins`, gated by the new opt-in config. **What was not tested:** Live exec-approval prompts against a real shell session under `tools.exec.security = "allowlist"` to visually confirm the prompt-suppression UX is what operators expect. Not feasible without burning a real OpenClaw approval flow against a configured agent. The production code path from `evaluateShellAllowlist` downward is exactly what the approval gate calls; the probe in step 1 invokes that same exported function with the same shape of inputs, so the production decision is identical between the probe and a live run.

## User-visible behavior change

**Default off — no change for existing deployments.** Operators who do not configure `tools.exec.safeBuiltins` see the same exec-approval behavior as before.

**Opt-in widens the gate** for the four conservative-default builtins (`:`, `false`, `pwd`, `true`) when configured. Operators who want to auto-allow chained commands starting with `cd`, `export`, or `unset` can add them explicitly to the config with the cwd/env-mutation trade-off documented in `docs/tools/exec-approvals-advanced.md`.

**Misconfiguration**: a typo or accidental configuration of `eval`, `source`, `.`, or any unrelated binary name in `tools.exec.safeBuiltins` is silently dropped at normalization rather than enabling the unsafe builtin. There is no error or warning — the operator sees no behavior change for those entries.

## Security impact

The fix **narrows** the implementation's exposure surface relative to the original PR diff that clawsweeper reviewed:

- **Closed supported set**: `tools.exec.safeBuiltins` cannot be used to enable arbitrary builtin names. The closed-set check runs at config normalization time, before the allowlist evaluator ever sees the value. `eval`, `source`, and `.` — the builtins that evaluate arbitrary shell code — are unreachable through this config.
- **Windows / PowerShell isolation**: `isSafeBuiltinSegment` returns false on Windows hosts. The opt-in cannot accidentally widen PowerShell behavior where the same token names have different semantics.
- **Resolved-binary defer**: `isSafeBuiltinSegment` returns false when the segment resolves to a real binary on disk (e.g., `/usr/bin/pwd` exists). The feature only auto-allows true shell builtins, not the GNU coreutils binaries with the same names.
- **No new privileged surface**, no payload mutation, no change to redaction or sandbox enforcement. The diff is purely additive at the allowlist-decision boundary.

The remaining maintainer decision flagged by clawsweeper — whether to ship this new exec-approval policy knob at all — stands. The closed-set normalization makes the knob defensible: there is no configuration shape that opens a code-evaluation bypass.
